What could make a town full of rodents suddenly freeze up?Things only get weirder, though, when the town is suddenly visited by a flying saucer--full of talking cats! But when the town's cows start disappearing mysteriously, Jilly suspects the visitors may be up to no good. Now she just has to prove it.\"]", "description": "[]", "price": "7.43", "store": "Dr. Roach (Author)", "categories": "[\"Books\", \"Children's Books\", \"Science Fiction & Fantasy\"]", "details": "This book, published by Scholastic Paperbacks in a reprint edition on October 29, 2013, is written in English and consists of 64 pages. Her father would write down all of the stories she told him and they would read them together. Jessica wants to continue the tradition of making up stories for children, starting with her debut picture book, The Old Man and the Pirate Princess. Jessica wants to be able to work with schools, libraries, and after care programs to help children discover their love of reading, writing, and creating. She wants her books to inspire others as much as her favorite books inspired her when she was growing up. Jessica also publishes adult fiction under her pen name, J.L. Mathews. She hopes her work will inspire others to create something of their own to share with the world. Jessica is an active member of her local writers guild in St. Louis where she lives with her two cats, Storm and Knight. She recommends every writer join a literary organization to network with other writers and find their people. She also recommends adopting as many cats as you can.\"]}", "rating_number": "5", "features": "[\"Pirate Princess Saria learns a valuable lesson alongside her plucky pirate parrot, Poppy, when they go to Buccaneer Bob's and the unexpected happens. Since the Caterpillar was published in 1969, Eric Carle has illustrated more than 70 books, many best sellers, most of which he also wrote.\", \"Carle says: \\\"With many of my books I attempt to bridge the gap between the home and school. To me home represents, or should represent; warmth, security, toys, holding hands, being held. School is a strange and new place for a child. Will it be a happy place? There are new people, a teacher, classmates - will they be friendly? I believe the passage from home to school is the second biggest trauma of childhood; the first is, of course, being born. Indeed, in both cases we leave a place of warmth and protection for one that is unknown. The unknown often brings fear with it. In my books I try to counteract this fear, to replace it with a positive message. I believe that children are naturally creative and eager to learn. I want to show them that learning is really both fascinating and fun.\\\"\", \"Eric Carle has two grown-up children, a son and a daughter. result.preview:
[{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

Plan on making most of the projects.", "review_time": "2020-02-27 05:11:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_76"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Heroine blames others for things & feels her bad behavior is justified", "text": "\"Dead Silence\" is a Christian suspense novel. There was no romance. When Elsie realized that the FBI had no leads except for the ones that she gave them, she and her sister-in-law and a female reporter and a female lawyer worked together to find the clues and solve the case. Granted, they did get a little help from two men, but the women were the ones actively solving the case. The FBI were portrayed as hampered by having to follow protocol and sometimes as downright incompetent. This created some suspense as the bad guys could leave threatening messages with little fear of being caught.

Unfortunately, I didn't really like Elise. She tended to forget important things whenever her son was threatened, so it was easy to throw her off her game. When one of the FBI agents pointed out that the senator died because Elise forgot to warn her or the police about the plot, Elise kicked the agents out and refused to provide them with the information they requested that would help catch the bad guy who murdered her mother-in-law and was threatening her. She did show courage by continuing to investigate the case even when threatened. But when she and one of the FBI agents were talking in the house, something happened outside that they equally should have noticed but she publicly blamed the FBI for not seeing it. She promised to do certain things that would prevent a sympathetic FBI agent from getting in trouble for helping her, but then she didn't do them and made his life very difficult. She used her deep love for her son to justify her behavior, but one can love their child without also blaming everyone else for their own mistakes.

A major clue was dropped at the beginning, allowing the reader to know all along who the bad guy was. It was understandable that the main characters didn't immediately see the significance, but it still took them a frustratingly long time to make the connections. Elise was angry at God for allowing her son to be born deaf, his father and grandmother killed, etc. Her sister-in-law encouraged her to talk to God and trust him. There was no sex or bad language.

Looking forward to the next book.", "review_time": "2021-07-31 18:34:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_23"}, {"rating": "2", "title": "Overpriced for the size", "text": "Book is small and feels cheap. I thought it was going to be a bigger book for kiddos.", "review_time": "2021-01-27 07:08:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_99"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Murder, Kidnapping, and Prayers", "text": "There are so many things to love about \"Dead Silence,\" by Robin Caroll. First and foremost, I absolutely adore the protagonist, Elise Carmichael, and her 7 year-old son Sawyer. Elise is a widow, whose husband was killed in military action. Once an aspiring model, she serves as a courtroom interpreter and devotes herself to caring for her deaf and mute son. Other major characters that Caroll endeared to me were Elise's sister-in-law, step father-in-law, babysitter, and a rookie reporter.

I enjoy books that use a lot of character dialog to advance the plot. It's even better when there is no foul language. For readers who find comfort in religion in times of trouble, this book is for you. Elise admits to being disappointed with, even mad at, God after losing her husband. During a series of traumatic events, she struggles to find her way back to her faith. Toward that end, she receives encouragement and counsel from her devout sister-in-law. Specific prayers and Bible verses are quoted. Blackmail, kidnapping, leaks from the FBI, self-serving politicians, and a dramatic rescue attempt spice up the story.

The only reservations I have about \"Dead Silence\" are some incredibly delayed responses by Elise in the early parts of the action. Although her son's accident certainly provides a plausible distraction, Elise literally ignores a death threat of which she had become aware. Later, she puts off reviewing an email that was highly likely to contain clues about a murder. She gets her act together by the middle of the book and becomes an active participant instead of a hapless bystander. Inasmuch as Elise's hesitations are important to the plot, and because she is so lovable, I am willing to overlook the almost unbelievable poor judgment on her part to recommend this book.", "review_time": "2020-06-14 11:52:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_167"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "A Most Entertaining and enjoyable book.", "text": "There were so many twists and turns to this book. args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load children's books data
childrens_books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(childrens_books_path, 'r') as f:
    childrens_books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews from 2020 onwards
reviews_2020_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']
with open(reviews_2020_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create dataframes
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

# Convert rating to numeric
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'])

# Merge books with reviews (book_id in books matches purchase_id in reviews)
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_id', right_on='purchase_id', how='inner')

# Group by book and calculate average rating
book_ratings = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Filter books with average rating >= 4.5
high_rated_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] >= 4.5].copy()

# Sort by average rating (descending) and then by review count (descending)
high_rated_books = high_rated_books.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])

# Prepare result
result = high_rated_books[['title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')

# Format average rating to 2 decimal places
for book in result:
    book['avg_rating'] = round(book['avg_rating'], 2)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load children's books data
childrens_books_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
with open(childrens_books_path, 'r') as f:
    childrens_books = json.load(f)

# Load reviews from 2020 onwards
reviews_2020_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']
with open(reviews_2020_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create dataframes
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

# Convert rating to numeric
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'])

# Merge books with reviews (book_id in books matches purchase_id in reviews)
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_id', right_on='purchase_id', how='inner')

# Group by book and calculate average rating
book_ratings = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Filter books with average rating >= 4.5
high_rated_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] >= 4.5].copy()

# Sort by average rating (descending) and then by review count (descending)
high_rated_books = high_rated_books.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])

# Prepare result
result = high_rated_books[['title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')

# Format average rating to 2 decimal places
for book in result:
    book['avg_rating'] = round(book['avg_rating'], 2)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

import json
import pandas as pd

# Debug: check what type of data we have
print('Type of var_functions.query_db:12:', type(locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']))
print('Type of var_functions.query_db:16:', type(locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']))

# The data might already be loaded as lists
childrens_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_2020 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

# Check if they're already lists or if they're file paths
if isinstance(childrens_books, str) and childrens_books.endswith('.json'):
    with open(childrens_books, 'r') as f:
        childrens_books = json.load(f)

if isinstance(reviews_2020, str) and reviews_2020.endswith('.json'):
    with open(reviews_2020, 'r') as f:
        reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create dataframes
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

print('Books dataframe shape:', books_df.shape)
print('Reviews dataframe shape:', reviews_df.shape)
print('Books columns:', books_df.columns.tolist())
print('Reviews columns:', reviews_df.columns.tolist())

# Show sample data
print('\nSample books:')
print(books_df.head())
print('\nSample reviews:')
print(reviews_df.head())

result = {'status': 'debug_complete', 'books_count': len(books_df), 'reviews_count': len(reviews_df)}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

childrens_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_2020 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'])

merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_id', right_on='purchase_id', how='inner')

book_ratings = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

high_rated_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] >= 4.5].copy()
high_rated_books = high_rated_books.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])

result = high_rated_books[['title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')

for book in result:
    book['avg_rating'] = round(book['avg_rating'], 2)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Check the actual content of the variables
childrens_books_var = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_2020_var = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

# The variables might already contain the data (not file paths)
if isinstance(childrens_books_var, list):
    childrens_books = childrens_books_var
else:
    # It's a file path, load it
    with open(childrens_books_var, 'r') as f:
        childrens_books = json.load(f)

if isinstance(reviews_2020_var, list):
    reviews_2020 = reviews_2020_var
else:
    # It's a file path, load it
    with open(reviews_2020_var, 'r') as f:
        reviews_2020 = json.load(f)

# Create dataframes
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

# Convert rating to numeric
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'])

# Debug prints
print('Books shape:', books_df.shape)
print('Reviews shape:', reviews_df.shape)
print('Books columns:', books_df.columns.tolist())
print('Reviews columns:', reviews_df.columns.tolist())

# Filter and group
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_id', right_on='purchase_id', how='inner')
print('Merged shape:', merged_df.shape)

if merged_df.empty:
    print('No matches found between books and reviews')
else:
    book_ratings = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
    book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']
    
    high_rated_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] >= 4.5].copy()
    high_rated_books = high_rated_books.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])
    
    result = high_rated_books[['title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']].to_dict('records')
    
    for book in result:
        book['avg_rating'] = round(book['avg_rating'], 2)
    
    print('__RESULT__:')
    print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

reviews_df['purchase_num'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['purchase_num'])

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load data
childrens_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_2020 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

# Create dataframes
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

# Extract numeric part for matching
books_df['book_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract('(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['review_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract('(\d+)').astype(int)

# Merge
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_num', right_on='review_num', how='inner')

# Process ratings
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'])
merged_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(merged_df['rating'])

# Group by book
book_ratings = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Filter
high_rated_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] >= 4.5].copy()
high_rated_books = high_rated_books.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])

result = []
for _, book in high_rated_books.iterrows():
    result.append({'title': book['title'], 'avg_rating': round(book['avg_rating'], 2), 'review_count': int(book['review_count'])})

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

val_args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load data
childrens_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_2020 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

# Create dataframes
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

# Extract numeric part for matching
books_df['book_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract('(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['review_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract('(\d+)').astype(int)

# Merge
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_num', right_on='review_num', how='inner')

# Process ratings
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'])
merged_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(merged_df['rating'])

# Group by book
book_ratings = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Filter
high_rated_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] >= 4.5].copy()
high_rated_books = high_rated_books.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])

result = []
for _, book in high_rated_books.iterrows():
    result.append({'title': book['title'], 'avg_rating': round(book['avg_rating'], 2), 'review_count': int(book['review_count'])})

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

import json
import pandas as pd

# Load the data first
childrens_books_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_2020_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

# Check what we actually have
print('Type of childrens_books_data:', type(childrens_books_data))
print('Type of reviews_2020_data:', type(reviews_2020_data))

# If they're strings (file paths), load them
if isinstance(childrens_books_data, str):
    with open(childrens_books_data, 'r') as f:
        childrens_books_data = json.load(f)
        
if isinstance(reviews_2020_data, str):
    with open(reviews_2020_data, 'r') as f:
        reviews_2020_data = json.load(f)

print('After loading:')
print('Type of childrens_books_data:', type(childrens_books_data))
print('Type of reviews_2020_data:', type(reviews_2020_data))

# Check if they're lists
print('Is childrens_books_data a list?', isinstance(childrens_books_data, list))
print('Is reviews_2020_data a list?', isinstance(reviews_2020_data, list))

# Try to see first few items
if isinstance(childrens_books_data, list) and len(childrens_books_data) > 0:
    print('First childrens book:', childrens_books_data[0])

if isinstance(reviews_2020_data, list) and len(reviews_2020_data) > 0:
    print('First review:', reviews_2020_data[0])

# Now create dataframes
try:
    books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books_data)
    print('Books DataFrame created successfully, shape:', books_df.shape)
    print('Columns:', books_df.columns.tolist())
except Exception as e:
    print('Error creating books DataFrame:', e)

try:
    reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020_data)
    print('Reviews DataFrame created successfully, shape:', reviews_df.shape)
    print('Columns:', reviews_df.columns.tolist())
except Exception as e:
    print('Error creating reviews DataFrame:', e)

result = {'debug': 'complete'}
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load children's books data
childrens_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books)

# Load reviews from 2020 onwards  
reviews_2020 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020)

# Extract numeric IDs for matching
books_df['book_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract('(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['review_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract('(\d+)').astype(int)

# Merge on numeric IDs
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_num', right_on='review_num', how='inner')

# Convert rating to numeric and calculate average per book
merged_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(merged_df['rating'])
book_ratings = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
book_ratings.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Filter for books with avg rating >= 4.5
high_rated_books = book_ratings[book_ratings['avg_rating'] >= 4.5]
high_rated_books = high_rated_books.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])

# Prepare result
result = []
for _, book in high_rated_books.iterrows():
    result.append({
        'title': book['title'],
        'avg_rating': round(book['avg_rating'], 2),
        'review_count': int(book['review_count'])
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Get the data from local variables
childrens_books_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:12']
reviews_2020_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']

# Create dataframes
books_df = pd.DataFrame(childrens_books_data)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_2020_data)

# Extract numeric IDs
books_df['book_num'] = books_df['book_id'].apply(lambda x: int(re.search(r'bookid_(\d+)', x).group(1)))
reviews_df['purchase_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].apply(lambda x: int(re.search(r'purchaseid_(\d+)', x).group(1)))

# Convert rating to numeric
reviews_df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(reviews_df['rating'])

# Merge on numeric IDs
merged_df = pd.merge(books_df, reviews_df, left_on='book_num', right_on='purchase_num', how='inner')

# Group by book and calculate average rating
book_stats = merged_df.groupby(['book_id', 'title']).agg({'rating': ['mean', 'count']}).reset_index()
book_stats.columns = ['book_id', 'title', 'avg_rating', 'review_count']

# Filter for books with avg rating >= 4.5
high_rated = book_stats[book_stats['avg_rating'] >= 4.5].copy()
high_rated = high_rated.sort_values(['avg_rating', 'review_count'], ascending=[False, False])

# Format result
result = []
for _, row in high_rated.iterrows():
    result.append({
        'title': row['title'],
        'avg_rating': round(row['avg_rating'], 2),
        'review_count': int(row['review_count'])
    })

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json

# Load the data
try:
    childrens_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:36']
    reviews_2020 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:16']
    
    # Check data types
    print('Childrens books type:', type(childrens_books))
    print('Reviews 2020 type:', type(reviews_2020))
    
    # Check first few items to understand structure
    if isinstance(childrens_books, list) and len(childrens_books) > 0:
        print('First children book:', childrens_books[0])
    
    if isinstance(reviews_2020, list) and len(reviews_2020) > 0:
        print('First review:', reviews_2020[0])
    
    # Check counts
    print('Total childrens books:', len(childrens_books))
    print('Total reviews from 2020+:', len(reviews_2020))
    
    # Show some book IDs
    print('Book IDs:', [b['book_id'] for b in childrens_books[:5]])
    
    # Show some review purchase IDs

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json

# Get the data
books_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:42']
reviews_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:44']

# Create a mapping of book_num to title
book_titles = {}
for book in books_data:
    book_titles[int(book['book_num'])] = book['title']

# Group reviews by book and calculate average rating
from collections import defaultdict
book_reviews = defaultdict(list)

for review in reviews_data:
    # Extract the numeric part from purchase_id
    purchase_num = int(review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1])
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    book_reviews[purchase_num].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings
result = []
for book_num, ratings in book_reviews.items():
    if book_num in book_titles:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            result.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_num],
                'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

# Sort by average rating (descending) and then by review count (descending)
result.sort(key=lambda x: (x['avg_rating'], x['review_count']), reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: Four Stars
var_functions.query_db:8.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_functions.query_db:8.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_functions.query_db:8.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json

# Load children's books and reviews from 2020+
childrens_books = locals()['var_functions.query_db:48']
reviews_2020 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:44']

# Create mapping of book number to title
book_titles = {}
for book in childrens_books:
    book_num = int(book['book_id'].split('_')[1])
    book_titles[book_num] = book['title']

# Group reviews by book and calculate average rating
from collections import defaultdict
book_ratings = defaultdict(list)

for review in reviews_2020:
    # Extract book number from purchase_id (e.g., "purchaseid_4" -> 4)
    book_num = int(review['purchase_id'].split('_')[1])
    rating = float(review['rating'])
    book_ratings[book_num].append(rating)

# Calculate average ratings and filter for >= 4.5
result = []
for book_num, ratings in book_ratings.items():
    if book_num in book_titles:
        avg_rating = sum(ratings) / len(ratings)
        if avg_rating >= 4.5:
            result.append({
                'title': book_titles[book_num],
                'avg_rating': round(avg_rating, 2),
                'review_count': len(ratings)
            })

# Sort by average rating (descending) and then by review count (descending)
result.sort(key=lambda x: (x['avg_rating'], x['review_count']), reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
